narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Wednesday, July 1, 2026
Dangerous Heat Continues Through Friday
Saharan Dust Creates Hazy Skies Across the Region
Storm Chances Increase for the Independence Day Weekend

The First Warn Weather Alert remains in effect as dangerous heat continues across Missouri and Illinois. Heat Warnings and Heat Advisories remain in place through Friday, with afternoon heat index values ranging from 100 to 110 degrees. This prolonged stretch of heat is becoming one of the longest periods of dangerous summer weather we've experienced this year, and the combination of high temperatures and oppressive humidity will continue to create hazardous conditions for anyone spending extended time outdoors.

Today marks another day of relentless heat as a strong ridge of high pressure remains firmly in control across the eastern half of the country. Afternoon temperatures will climb into the upper 90s, and while slightly lower humidity may mix down during the hottest part of the afternoon, it will do little to reduce the overall danger. Heat index values will once again soar well above 100 degrees, making heat-related illnesses a significant concern. If you must be outdoors, drink plenty of water, take frequent breaks in the shade or air conditioning, wear light-colored clothing, and never leave children or pets inside parked vehicles.

Adding to the summer feel is a veil of Saharan dust drifting across the Mississippi Valley. This plume of dust originated thousands of miles away over the Sahara Desert in North Africa before crossing the Atlantic Ocean and Gulf of Mexico. While the dust remains high in the atmosphere and is not expected to create significant air quality concerns for most people, it will produce noticeably hazy skies, colorful sunrises and sunsets, and may slightly reduce visibility at times. Those with asthma or other respiratory conditions may notice minor irritation during periods of higher dust concentrations.

Thursday will bring much of the same weather pattern, although the atmosphere will become a little less stable as the ridge gradually weakens. Isolated afternoon and evening thunderstorms may develop during the peak heating of the day. These will be typical summertime "pop-up" storms, producing brief heavy downpours, frequent lightning, and gusty winds. Coverage will remain very limited, however, and most communities will stay dry. Any cooling from these storms will be temporary and localized, offering little relief from the persistent heat.

By Friday, the upper-level ridge begins to weaken further, allowing for a few more scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. Temperatures may ease back a degree or two, but it will remain hot and humid with heat index values continuing to approach or exceed 100 degrees. Heat headlines are expected to remain in effect through at least Friday, and some may need to be extended into the holiday weekend depending on how temperatures and cloud cover evolve.

Looking ahead to Independence Day, confidence continues to increase that scattered showers and thunderstorms will become more common as the weather pattern becomes more active. While the day certainly won't be a washout, periods of rain and thunderstorms are expected to develop, especially during the afternoon and evening hours. The exact timing and coverage remain uncertain, but anyone with outdoor holiday plans should keep a close eye on the forecast as details become clearer over the next couple of days.

The good news is that after Saturday, the likelihood of extreme heat begins to decrease. While temperatures will remain warm and humid, a more active weather pattern should help prevent the dangerous, prolonged heat that we've experienced throughout the week. Even so, summer is far from over, and residents should continue practicing heat safety until this extended heat wave finally breaks.

Stay with the First Warn Weather Team for the latest forecast updates, heat safety information, and any changes to the holiday weekend forecast. We remain First. Accurate. Trusted.
